question:
Prove that, for any two bounded functions $g_1, g_2: \RR \to [1, \infty)$,
there exist functions $h_1, h_2: \RR \to \RR$ such that, for every $x \in \RR$,
\[
\sup_{s \in \RR} (g_1(s)^x g_2(s))  = \max_{t \in \RR} (x h_1(t) + h_2(t)).
\]
solution:
Define the function
\[
f(x) = \sup_{s \in \RR} \{x \log g_1(s) + \log g_2(s)\}.
\]
As a function of $x$, $f$ is the supremum of a collection of affine functions, so it is convex.
The function $e^{f(x)}$ is then also convex, as may be checked directly from the definition:
for $x_1, x_2 \in \RR$ and $t \in [0,1]$, by the weighted AM-GM inequality
\begin{align*}
t e^{f(x_1)} + (1-t) e^{f(x_2)}&\geq e^{t f(x_1) + (1-t)f(x_2)} \\
&\geq e^{f(t x_1 + (1-t)x_2)}.
\end{align*}
For each $t \in \RR$, draw a supporting line to the graph of $e^{f(x)}$ at $x=t$;
it has the form $y = x h_1(t) + h_2(t)$ for some $h_1(t), h_2(t) \in \RR$. For all $x$, we then have
\[
\sup_{s \in \RR} \{g_1(s)^x g_2(s) \} \geq x h_1(t) + h_2(t)
\]
with equality for $x = t$. This proves the desired equality (including the fact that the maximum on the right side is achieved).

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
This problem demonstrates an example of \emph{duality} for convex functions.

descriptive_long.question:
Prove that, for any two bounded functions $firstbounded, secondbounded: \RR \to [1, \infty)$, there exist functions $firstsupport, secondsupport: \RR \to \RR$ such that, for every $variable \in \RR$,\[
\sup_{argument \in \RR} (firstbounded(argument)^{variable} secondbounded(argument))  = \max_{parameter \in \RR} (variable firstsupport(parameter) + secondsupport(parameter)).
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Define the function
\[
convexfunction(variable) = \sup_{argument \in \RR} \{variable \log firstbounded(argument) + \log secondbounded(argument)\}.
\]
As a function of $variable$, $convexfunction$ is the supremum of a collection of affine functions, so it is convex.
The function $e^{convexfunction(variable)}$ is then also convex, as may be checked directly from the definition:
for $variable_{1}, variable_{2} \in \RR$ and $parameter \in [0,1]$, by the weighted AM-GM inequality
\begin{align*}
parameter e^{convexfunction(variable_{1})} + (1-parameter) e^{convexfunction(variable_{2})}&\geq e^{parameter \, convexfunction(variable_{1}) + (1-parameter)\,convexfunction(variable_{2})} \\
&\geq e^{convexfunction(parameter \, variable_{1} + (1-parameter)\,variable_{2})}.
\end{align*}
For each $parameter \in \RR$, draw a supporting line to the graph of $e^{convexfunction(variable)}$ at $variable=parameter$;
it has the form $ordinate = variable firstsupport(parameter) + secondsupport(parameter)$ for some $firstsupport(parameter), secondsupport(parameter) \in \RR$. For all $variable$, we then have
\[
\sup_{argument \in \RR} \{firstbounded(argument)^{variable} secondbounded(argument) \} \geq variable firstsupport(parameter) + secondsupport(parameter)
\]
with equality for $variable = parameter$. This proves the desired equality (including the fact that the maximum on the right side is achieved).

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
This problem demonstrates an example of \emph{duality} for convex functions.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Prove that, for any two bounded functions $waterfall, moonlight: \RR \to [1, \infty)$,
there exist functions $arrowhead, windchime: \RR \to \RR$ such that, for every $sandcastle \in \RR$,
\[
\sup_{lanternfish \in \RR} (waterfall(lanternfish)^{sandcastle} moonlight(lanternfish))  = \max_{parchment \in \RR} (sandcastle \, arrowhead(parchment) + windchime(parchment)).
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Define the function
\[
drumbeat(sandcastle) = \sup_{lanternfish \in \RR} \{sandcastle \log waterfall(lanternfish) + \log moonlight(lanternfish)\}.
\]
As a function of $sandcastle$, $drumbeat$ is the supremum of a collection of affine functions, so it is convex.
The function $e^{drumbeat(sandcastle)}$ is then also convex, as may be checked directly from the definition:
for $sandcastle_1, sandcastle_2 \in \RR$ and $parchment \in [0,1]$, by the weighted AM-GM inequality
\begin{align*}
parchment \, e^{drumbeat(sandcastle_1)} + (1-parchment) \, e^{drumbeat(sandcastle_2)}&\ge e^{parchment \, drumbeat(sandcastle_1) + (1-parchment)drumbeat(sandcastle_2)} \\
&\ge e^{drumbeat(parchment \, sandcastle_1 + (1-parchment)sandcastle_2)}.
\end{align*}
For each $parchment \in \RR$, draw a supporting line to the graph of $e^{drumbeat(sandcastle)}$ at $sandcastle=parchment$;
it has the form $\silhouette = sandcastle \, arrowhead(parchment) + windchime(parchment)$ for some $arrowhead(parchment), windchime(parchment) \in \RR$. For all $sandcastle$, we then have
\[
\sup_{lanternfish \in \RR} \{waterfall(lanternfish)^{sandcastle} moonlight(lanternfish) \} \ge sandcastle \, arrowhead(parchment) + windchime(parchment)
\]
with equality for $sandcastle = parchment$. This proves the desired equality (including the fact that the maximum on the right side is achieved).

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
This problem demonstrates an example of \emph{duality} for convex functions.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Prove that, for any two bounded functions $shrinkerone, shrinkertwo: \RR \to [1, \infty)$,
there exist functions $undermineone, underminetwo: \RR \to \RR$ such that, for every $immutable \in \RR$,
\[
\sup_{steadfast \in \RR} (shrinkerone(steadfast)^{immutable} shrinkertwo(steadfast))  = \max_{timeless \in \RR} (immutable\; undermineone(timeless) + underminetwo(timeless)).
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Define the function
\[
constant(immutable) = \sup_{steadfast \in \RR} \{ immutable \log shrinkerone(steadfast) + \log shrinkertwo(steadfast) \}.
\]
As a function of $immutable$, $constant$ is the supremum of a collection of affine functions, so it is convex.
The function $e^{constant(immutable)}$ is then also convex, as may be checked directly from the definition:
for $x_1, x_2 \in \RR$ and $timeless \in [0,1]$, by the weighted AM-GM inequality
\begin{align*}
 timeless e^{constant(x_1)} + (1-timeless) e^{constant(x_2)} &\geq e^{timeless constant(x_1) + (1-timeless)constant(x_2)} \\
 &\geq e^{constant(timeless x_1 + (1-timeless)x_2)}.
\end{align*}
For each $timeless \in \RR$, draw a supporting line to the graph of $e^{constant(immutable)}$ at $immutable=timeless$;
it has the form $grounded = immutable\; undermineone(timeless) + underminetwo(timeless)$ for some $undermineone(timeless),\; underminetwo(timeless) \in \RR$. For all $immutable$, we then have
\[
\sup_{steadfast \in \RR} \{ shrinkerone(steadfast)^{immutable} shrinkertwo(steadfast) \} \geq immutable\; undermineone(timeless) + underminetwo(timeless)
\]
with equality for $immutable = timeless$. This proves the desired equality (including the fact that the maximum on the right side is achieved).

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
This problem demonstrates an example of \emph{duality} for convex functions.

garbled_string.question:
Prove that, for any two bounded functions $qzxwvtnp, hjgtrsla: \RR \to [1, \infty)$,
there exist functions $mnbvcxzq, plmoknij: \RR \to \RR$ such that, for every $klmqpers \in \RR$,
\[
\sup_{fdjsklwe \in \RR} (qzxwvtnp(fdjsklwe)^{klmqpers} hjgtrsla(fdjsklwe))  = \max_{ghrplmno \in \RR} (klmqpers mnbvcxzq(ghrplmno) + plmoknij(ghrplmno)).
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Define the function
\[
bvnzxcqe(klmqpers) = \sup_{fdjsklwe \in \RR} \{klmqpers \log qzxwvtnp(fdjsklwe) + \log hjgtrsla(fdjsklwe)\}.
\]
As a function of $klmqpers$, $bvnzxcqe$ is the supremum of a collection of affine functions, so it is convex.
The function $e^{bvnzxcqe(klmqpers)}$ is then also convex, as may be checked directly from the definition:
for $klmqpers_1, klmqpers_2 \in \RR$ and $ghrplmno \in [0,1]$, by the weighted AM-GM inequality
\begin{align*}
ghrplmno e^{bvnzxcqe(klmqpers_1)} + (1-ghrplmno) e^{bvnzxcqe(klmqpers_2)}&\geq e^{ghrplmno bvnzxcqe(klmqpers_1) + (1-ghrplmno)bvnzxcqe(klmqpers_2)} \\
&\geq e^{bvnzxcqe(ghrplmno klmqpers_1 + (1-ghrplmno)klmqpers_2)}.
\end{align*}
For each $ghrplmno \in \RR$, draw a supporting line to the graph of $e^{bvnzxcqe(klmqpers)}$ at $klmqpers=ghrplmno$;
it has the form $lskdjfue = klmqpers mnbvcxzq(ghrplmno) + plmoknij(ghrplmno)$ for some $mnbvcxzq(ghrplmno), plmoknij(ghrplmno) \in \RR$. For all $klmqpers$, we then have
\[
\sup_{fdjsklwe \in \RR} \{qzxwvtnp(fdjsklwe)^{klmqpers} hjgtrsla(fdjsklwe) \} \geq klmqpers mnbvcxzq(ghrplmno) + plmoknij(ghrplmno)
\]
with equality for $klmqpers = ghrplmno$. This proves the desired equality (including the fact that the maximum on the right side is achieved).

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
This problem demonstrates an example of \emph{duality} for convex functions.

kernel_variant.question:
Let k \geq  2 and d be positive integers and let A be a k \times  d real matrix of full row-rank.  
Assume that the linear system A s = c is consistent, i.e. the affine sub-space  

  S := {s \in  \mathbb{R}^d : A s = c}                                                                                                          (0)

is non-empty.  
Let  

  g_0,\ldots ,g_k : \mathbb{R}^d \to  [2,1000]

be arbitrary bounded Borel maps and, for x = (x_1,\ldots ,x_k) \in  \mathbb{R}^k, define  

  F(x) := sup_{s\in S} g_0(s)\cdot g_1(s)^{x_1}\cdots g_k(s)^{x_k},                                          (\star )  
  f := log F.

For s \in  S introduce the logarithmic coordinates  

  \alpha (s) := (log g_1(s),\ldots ,log g_k(s)) \in  \mathbb{R}^k, \beta (s) := log g_0(s) \in  \mathbb{R}.                  (1)

Because 2 \leq  g_j \leq  1000 we have  

  \|\alpha (s)\|_2 \leq  \sqrt{k} log 1000, |\beta (s)| \leq  log 1000 =: L.                            (2)

Problem  

(i)  Show that f is finite-valued, convex and globally Lipschitz on \mathbb{R}^k.  
     (Give an explicit Lipschitz constant.)  

(ii) Put   T := cl{(\alpha (s),\beta (s)): s\in S} \subset  \mathbb{R}^{k+1}.                                       (3)

     Prove that T is compact and that  

  f(x) = max_{(a,b)\in T} (a\cdot x + b) for every x \in  \mathbb{R}^k.                        (\dagger )

(iii) (Uniform polyhedral approximation)  
      For every compact K \subset  \mathbb{R}^k and \varepsilon  > 0 there exists a finite set  
      T_{K,\varepsilon } \subset  T such that  

  0 \leq  f(x) - max_{t\in T_{K,\varepsilon }}(a(t)\cdot x + b(t)) \leq  \varepsilon  for all x\in K.            (4)

(iv) (Quantitative version of (iii))  
      Let  

  M := sup_{s\in S}\|\alpha (s)\|_2 \leq  \sqrt{k} L, diam K := sup_{x,y\in K}\|x-y\|_2,

      and let C_k be a constant depending only on k.  
      Then one can choose T_{K,\varepsilon } so that  

  |T_{K,\varepsilon }| \leq  C_k ( M\cdot diam K / \varepsilon  )^k.                                         (5)

(v) (Polyhedrality criterion)  Denote by conv T the closed convex hull of T.  
    The following statements are equivalent.  

 (a) There exists a finite subset T_0 \subset  T with  
        f(x) = max_{t\in T_0}(a(t)\cdot x + b(t)) for all x\in \mathbb{R}^k.  

 (b) conv T is a polytope, i.e. the convex hull of finitely many points in \mathbb{R}^{k+1}.  

    Moreover, a sufficient (but not necessary) condition for (a)-(b) is  

 (c) There exists a finite subset S_0 \subset  S such that  
        conv T = conv{(\alpha (s),\beta (s)) : s\in S_0}.  

Tasks (iii)-(v) require both quantitative and qualitative tools from convex geometry.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout abbreviate  

 M := sup_{s\in S}\|\alpha (s)\|_2 \leq  \sqrt{k} L.                                                        (6)



Step 1. Logarithmic rewriting.  
Taking logarithms in (\star ) gives  

 f(x) = sup_{s\in S} (x\cdot \alpha (s) + \beta (s)),                                             (7)

so f is the point-wise supremum of affine functions of x.



2. Proof of (i).

Finiteness. For every x\in \mathbb{R}^k and s\in S, by (2)

 |x\cdot \alpha (s)+\beta (s)| \leq  \|x\|_2\|\alpha (s)\|_2 + |\beta (s)| \leq  \|x\|_2 M + L,

hence -\|x\|_2 M - L \leq  f(x) \leq  \|x\|_2 M + L, so f is finite everywhere.

Convexity. A supremum of affine functions is convex.

Lipschitzness. For x,y\in \mathbb{R}^k,

 |f(x)-f(y)| \leq  sup_{s\in S}|(x-y)\cdot \alpha (s)| \leq  M\|x-y\|_2,                         (8)

thus f is globally M-Lipschitz.



3. Compact parameter set and representation (\dagger ).

Boundedness of \alpha (s),\beta (s) from (2) implies boundedness of {(\alpha (s),\beta (s)) : s\in S}; its closure T is therefore compact.

For a fixed x the map (a,b)\mapsto a\cdot x+b is continuous, so

 f(x)=max_{(a,b)\in T}(a\cdot x+b)                                                       (by (7)),

establishing (\dagger ).



4. Uniform polyhedral approximation - proof of (iii).

Let K \subset  \mathbb{R}^k be compact and \varepsilon >0.  
Put \delta  := \varepsilon /(2M).  Because K is compact, finitely many closed balls  
B(x^i, \delta ) (i=1,\ldots ,N) cover K.  For each centre x^i pick (a^i,b^i)\in T such that  

 f(x^i)=a^i\cdot x^i+b^i.                                                            (9)

Set T_{K,\varepsilon }:={(a^i,b^i):1\leq i\leq N}.  
Fix x\in K and choose i with \|x-x^i\|_2\leq \delta .  Using (8) and (9):

 f(x)-(a^i\cdot x+b^i)  
 = [f(x)-f(x^i)] + [a^i\cdot x^i+b^i-a^i\cdot x-b^i]  
 \leq  M\|x-x^i\|_2 + \|a^i\|_2\|x-x^i\|_2  
 \leq  M\delta  + M\delta  = 2M\delta  = \varepsilon .                                                         (10)

Because every affine form on the right-hand side of (4) is one used in (7), we always have  

 max_{t\in T_{K,\varepsilon }}(a(t)\cdot x+b(t)) \leq  f(x).  

Combining with (10) proves (4).



5. Quantitative bound - proof of (iv).

Standard volumetric packing/covering shows that the minimal number N of closed balls of radius \delta  needed to cover a k-dimensional set of diameter diam K satisfies  

 N \leq  C_k (diam K / \delta )^k.                                                       (11)

With \delta  = \varepsilon /(2M) inequality (11) yields  

 |T_{K,\varepsilon }| = N \leq  C_k ( M\cdot diam K / \varepsilon  )^k,

which is precisely (5).



6. Polyhedrality criterion - proof of (v).

Introduce the support functions

 \sigma _T(x) := max_{t\in T} t\cdot (x,1), \sigma _{T_0}(x) := max_{t\in T_0} t\cdot (x,1).              (12)

Observe that t=(a,b) acts on (x,1) as a\cdot x + b, so condition (a) is exactly \sigma _T = \sigma _{T_0}.

(a) \Rightarrow  (b). The function \sigma _T is the support function of the closed convex set conv T.  
Since \sigma _T = \sigma _{T_0} and T_0 is finite, conv T_0 is a polytope.  
Closed convex sets with the same support function coincide, hence  

 conv T = conv T_0,

so conv T is a polytope.

(b) \Rightarrow  (a). Assume conv T = conv{t^1,\ldots ,t^m}.  
Put T_0 := {t^1,\ldots ,t^m}.  For every x \in  \mathbb{R}^k we have

 f(x) = \sigma _T(x) = \sigma _{T_0}(x) = max_{1\leq j\leq m} t^j\cdot (x,1),

yielding (a).

Sufficiency of (c). If there exists S_0 with conv T = conv{(\alpha (s),\beta (s)) : s\in S_0} then, setting  
T_0 := {(\alpha (s),\beta (s)) : s\in S_0}, we obtain conv T = conv T_0, hence \sigma _T = \sigma _{T_0} and (a) follows, so (b) holds as well.

Remark. The converse (b) \Rightarrow  (c) is false in general:  take k = 1, d = 2, A = (1 0), c = 0, so S = {(0,s_2):s_2\in \mathbb{R}}.  
Let g_0\equiv 1 and g_1(0,s_2)=2+e^{-|s_2|}.  Then T = [log 2, log 3] \times  {0}.  
Here conv T = T is a line segment (a polytope), yet no finite S_0 can satisfy (c).



7. Differentiability remark.

At every point x where f is differentiable the maximiser in (\dagger ) is unique and equals  

 (\nabla f(x), f(x) - \nabla f(x)\cdot x).

\blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Higher dimension & more variables:  The exponent now contains a k-tuple x (k ≥ 2) instead of a single real; the domain of maximisation is the affine subspace {s : A s=c} of ℝᵈ (d may be arbitrarily large).

2.  Extra constraints:  The maximisation is not over all s but over those satisfying A s=c, forcing the solver to cope with an external linear restriction.

3.  Deeper theory:  The solution must invoke
   •  vector–valued convex analysis (support functions of compact sets in ℝ^{k+1});  
   •  topological arguments guaranteeing attainment of suprema via compactification;  
   •  measurable selection theorems and δ–net (or Carathéodory–type) arguments to extract finite determining sets.

4.  Multiple interacting concepts:  The proof intertwines boundedness, compact closures, supporting hyperplanes, subgradients, Lipschitz estimates, and finite covering arguments.

5.  Additional conclusion (iii):  Producing a finite determining set on each compact K is absent from the original problem and forces the contestant to deploy a quantitative compactness argument beyond the basic supporting-line construction.

Altogether these extensions raise the problem well above the original in both technical breadth and conceptual depth; simple pattern matching no longer suffices.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let k \geq  2 and d be positive integers and let A be a k \times  d real matrix of full row-rank.  
Assume that the linear system A s = c is consistent, i.e. the affine sub-space

be arbitrary bounded Borel maps and, for x = (x_1,\ldots ,x_k) \in  \mathbb{R}^k, define  

  F(x) := sup_{s\in S} g_0(s)\cdot g_1(s)^{x_1}\cdots g_k(s)^{x_k},                                          (\star )

 (b) conv T is a polytope, i.e. the convex hull of finitely many points in \mathbb{R}^{k+1}.  

    Moreover, a sufficient (but not necessary) condition for (a)-(b) is  

 (c) There exists a finite subset S_0 \subset  S such that  
        conv T = conv{(\alpha (s),\beta (s)) : s\in S_0}.

Throughout abbreviate  

 M := sup_{s\in S}\|\alpha (s)\|_2 \leq  \sqrt{k} L.                                                        (6)



Step 1. Logarithmic rewriting.  
Taking logarithms in (\star ) gives  

 f(x) = sup_{s\in S} (x\cdot \alpha (s) + \beta (s)),                                             (7)

so f is the point-wise supremum of affine functions of x.



2. Proof of (i).

Finiteness. For every x\in \mathbb{R}^k and s\in S, by (2)

 |x\cdot \alpha (s)+\beta (s)| \leq  \|x\|_2\|\alpha (s)\|_2 + |\beta (s)| \leq  \|x\|_2 M + L,

hence -\|x\|_2 M - L \leq  f(x) \leq  \|x\|_2 M + L, so f is finite everywhere.

Lipschitzness. For x,y\in \mathbb{R}^k,

establishing (\dagger ).



4. Uniform polyhedral approximation - proof of (iii).

Let K \subset  \mathbb{R}^k be compact and \varepsilon >0.  
Put \delta  := \varepsilon /(2M).  Because K is compact, finitely many closed balls  
B(x^i, \delta ) (i=1,\ldots ,N) cover K.  For each centre x^i pick (a^i,b^i)\in T such that

Set T_{K,\varepsilon }:={(a^i,b^i):1\leq i\leq N}.  
Fix x\in K and choose i with \|x-x^i\|_2\leq \delta .  Using (8) and (9):

 f(x)-(a^i\cdot x+b^i)  
 = [f(x)-f(x^i)] + [a^i\cdot x^i+b^i-a^i\cdot x-b^i]

Because every affine form on the right-hand side of (4) is one used in (7), we always have

Combining with (10) proves (4).

which is precisely (5).

Introduce the support functions

 \sigma _T(x) := max_{t\in T} t\cdot (x,1), \sigma _{T_0}(x) := max_{t\in T_0} t\cdot (x,1).              (12)

Observe that t=(a,b) acts on (x,1) as a\cdot x + b, so condition (a) is exactly \sigma _T = \sigma _{T_0}.

(a) \Rightarrow  (b). The function \sigma _T is the support function of the closed convex set conv T.  
Since \sigma _T = \sigma _{T_0} and T_0 is finite, conv T_0 is a polytope.  
Closed convex sets with the same support function coincide, hence  

 conv T = conv T_0,

so conv T is a polytope.

(b) \Rightarrow  (a). Assume conv T = conv{t^1,\ldots ,t^m}.  
Put T_0 := {t^1,\ldots ,t^m}.  For every x \in  \mathbb{R}^k we have

 f(x) = \sigma _T(x) = \sigma _{T_0}(x) = max_{1\leq j\leq m} t^j\cdot (x,1),

yielding (a).

Sufficiency of (c). If there exists S_0 with conv T = conv{(\alpha (s),\beta (s)) : s\in S_0} then, setting  
T_0 := {(\alpha (s),\beta (s)) : s\in S_0}, we obtain conv T = conv T_0, hence \sigma _T = \sigma _{T_0} and (a) follows, so (b) holds as well.

Remark. The converse (b) \Rightarrow  (c) is false in general:  take k = 1, d = 2, A = (1 0), c = 0, so S = {(0,s_2):s_2\in \mathbb{R}}.  
Let g_0\equiv 1 and g_1(0,s_2)=2+e^{-|s_2|}.  Then T = [log 2, log 3] \times  {0}.  
Here conv T = T is a line segment (a polytope), yet no finite S_0 can satisfy (c).



7. Differentiability remark.

At every point x where f is differentiable the maximiser in (\dagger ) is unique and equals  

 (\nabla f(x), f(x) - \nabla f(x)\cdot x).

\blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Higher dimension & more variables:  The exponent now contains a k-tuple x (k ≥ 2) instead of a single real; the domain of maximisation is the affine subspace {s : A s=c} of ℝᵈ (d may be arbitrarily large).

2.  Extra constraints:  The maximisation is not over all s but over those satisfying A s=c, forcing the solver to cope with an external linear restriction.

3.  Deeper theory:  The solution must invoke
   •  vector–valued convex analysis (support functions of compact sets in ℝ^{k+1});  
   •  topological arguments guaranteeing attainment of suprema via compactification;  
   •  measurable selection theorems and δ–net (or Carathéodory–type) arguments to extract finite determining sets.

4.  Multiple interacting concepts:  The proof intertwines boundedness, compact closures, supporting hyperplanes, subgradients, Lipschitz estimates, and finite covering arguments.

5.  Additional conclusion (iii):  Producing a finite determining set on each compact K is absent from the original problem and forces the contestant to deploy a quantitative compactness argument beyond the basic supporting-line construction.

Altogether these extensions raise the problem well above the original in both technical breadth and conceptual depth; simple pattern matching no longer suffices.
